_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: Exceptions practice 1
choices: NumberFormatException
ClassCastException
IOException
NullPointerException
questions.explanation: The text does not match Java's integer format. The method reports that content problem with NumberFormatException.

questions.title: Exceptions practice 2
questions.code: String load(Path path) { /* reads a file */ }
questions.prompt: What must a method do with a checked IOException?
choices: Catch it or declare it with `throws`
Ignore it because all exceptions are optional
Convert it to an int
Place it in a static field
questions.explanation: Java's compiler tracks checked exceptions. The method must handle the IOException or state in its signature that a caller is responsible for it.

questions.title: Trace Exceptions
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
accepted: Credits must be a whole number
questions.explanation: The catch type matches the conversion failure. Code after the failed parse inside try is skipped, then execution continues after catch. The resulting output is 'Credits must be a whole number'.

questions.title: Exceptions good practice
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Exceptions?
choices: Catch a type that matches the recovery you can perform.
Skip input checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide every error and continue without checking the result.
Put all of the work in one long statement.
questions.explanation: Catch a type that matches the recovery you can perform. This keeps the code easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Exceptions practice 5
questions.prompt: Which version follows the lesson's safer approach?
choices: Both versions are equally safe.
Neither version can run.
questions.explanation: Catching Exception can mislabel a NullPointerException as bad input. The narrow catch handles only the case the message describes.

questions.title: Exceptions practice 6
questions.prompt: Which statement best explains 'Keep the cause when adding context'?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
If you create a new exception, pass the original one as its cause. Without that link, the stack trace loses the failure that started the chain and makes diagnosis harder.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: If you create a new exception, pass the original one as its cause. Without that link, the stack trace loses the failure that started the chain and makes diagnosis harder.

questions.title: Exceptions practice 7
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Exceptions?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Keep the protected block small.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Keep the protected block small. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Exceptions practice 8
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Exceptions?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
Distinguish checked exceptions from runtime exceptions.
questions.explanation: Distinguish checked exceptions from runtime exceptions. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Exceptions practice 9
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Exceptions?
choices: Preserve an original cause when wrapping.
Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Preserve an original cause when wrapping. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Exceptions practice 10
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Exceptions?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Use try-with-resources for closeable objects.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Use try-with-resources for closeable objects. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.
